---
title: "Warm-up: transpose, identity, symmetry"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

- Write the transpose $A^\top$ of a small matrix by flipping rows and columns.
- Recognise the identity matrix $I$ and see that $IA = A$.
- Recognise when a matrix is symmetric ($A = A^\top$).

## The rule

**Transpose.** $A^\top$ turns rows into columns. The entry in row $i$, column $j$ of $A$ becomes row $j$, column $i$ of $A^\top$. The diagonal stays put; everything else reflects across it.

$$
A=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
A^\top=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

Transposing twice gives back the original: $(A^\top)^\top = A$.

**Identity.** $I$ has 1s on the diagonal and 0s everywhere else. Multiplying by it changes nothing: $IA = A$ and $AI = A$.

$$
I=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Symmetric.** A matrix is **symmetric** when it equals its own transpose: $A = A^\top$. In picture form, it is a mirror image across the diagonal — the entry at row $i$, col $j$ equals the entry at row $j$, col $i$.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 \\ 5 & 9 \end{bmatrix}\text{ is symmetric}, \qquad
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 \\ 2 & 9 \end{bmatrix}\text{ is not.}
$$

## Drills

**Write the transpose:**

1. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}^\top$

2. $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 \\ 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}^\top$

3. $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}^\top$

4. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}^\top$ (a row becomes a column)

5. $\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}^\top$ (a column becomes a row)

6. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}^\top$

**Transpose twice ($(A^\top)^\top = A$):**

7. Take $A=\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. Write $A^\top$, then transpose that result. What do you get back?

**Multiply by the identity:**

8. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 7 \\ 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$

9. $\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$

10. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 9 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$

11. Without computing fully: what is $I\,\mathbf{x}$ when $\mathbf{x}=\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$ and $I$ is $3\times 3$?

**Is this matrix symmetric? (yes/no, and say why):**

12. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$

13. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$

14. $\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$

15. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 0 \\ 4 & 2 & 5 \\ 0 & 5 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$

16. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 0 \\ 4 & 2 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$

**A little harder:**

17. Fill in the missing entry so that $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 7 \\ \square & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ is symmetric.

18. Is the identity matrix $I$ symmetric? Explain in one line.

## Check in R

Transpose is [`t`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/t.html). The identity is built with [`diag`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/diag.html). To test symmetry, compare a matrix with its transpose using [`all`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/all.html) and `==`, or use [`isSymmetric`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/isSymmetric.html).

```r
A <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
t(A)
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    1    3
# [2,]    2    4
```

```r
diag(3)            # the 3x3 identity
#      [,1] [,2] [,3]
# [1,]    1    0    0
# [2,]    0    1    0
# [3,]    0    0    1
```

```r
S <- matrix(c(1, 2, 2, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
all(S == t(S))     # TRUE  -> symmetric
# [1] TRUE
isSymmetric(S)
# [1] TRUE
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — flip across the diagonal"}
To transpose, leave the diagonal entries alone and swap each off-diagonal pair: the entry at (row 1, col 2) trades places with (row 2, col 1).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — rows become columns"}
A $1\times 3$ row turns into a $3\times 1$ column, and vice versa. The numbers stay in the same order; only their orientation changes.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — the identity changes nothing"}
$I A = A$ and $A I = A$. You do not need to compute — the answer is just the other matrix, unchanged.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — symmetry test"}
Compare the entry at (row $i$, col $j$) with the one at (row $j$, col $i$). If every such pair is equal, the matrix is symmetric. A single mismatch means it is not.
:::

## Step-by-step solutions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solutions"}

**Drill 1.** Diagonal $1,4$ stays; swap the off-diagonal $2$ and $3$.
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 2.** Diagonal $0,0$ stays; swap $5$ (row 1, col 2) and $7$ (row 2, col 1).
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 7 \\ 5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 3.** Off-diagonal entries are both $0$, so swapping changes nothing.
$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}.$$
(This is a diagonal matrix; diagonal matrices are their own transpose.)

**Drill 4.** A $1\times 3$ row becomes a $3\times 1$ column.
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 5.** A $3\times 1$ column becomes a $1\times 3$ row.
$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 6.** A $2\times 3$ matrix becomes $3\times 2$. Row 1 $(1,2,3)$ becomes column 1; row 2 $(4,5,6)$ becomes column 2.
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 7.** $A=\begin{bmatrix}8&1\\0&2\end{bmatrix}$.
First transpose: $A^\top=\begin{bmatrix}8&0\\1&2\end{bmatrix}$.
Transpose again: $(A^\top)^\top=\begin{bmatrix}8&1\\0&2\end{bmatrix}=A$.
You get the original matrix back, illustrating $(A^\top)^\top = A$.

**Drill 8.** Multiplying by the identity on the left does nothing:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 7 \\ 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 9.** Multiplying by the identity on the right does nothing:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 10.** The $3\times 3$ identity times the column leaves it unchanged:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 9 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 11.** $I\,\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{x}$, so the answer is $\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$ — the identity leaves any vector unchanged.

**Drill 12.** Off-diagonal pair: (row 1, col 2) $=2$ and (row 2, col 1) $=2$. Equal, so **yes, symmetric**.

**Drill 13.** (row 1, col 2) $=2$ but (row 2, col 1) $=3$. Not equal, so **no, not symmetric**.

**Drill 14.** Off-diagonal entries are both $0$. They match, so **yes, symmetric** (every diagonal matrix is symmetric).

**Drill 15.** Check the three off-diagonal pairs: $(1,2)\!=\!4=(2,1)$; $(1,3)\!=\!0=(3,1)$; $(2,3)\!=\!5=(3,2)$. All match, so **yes, symmetric**.

**Drill 16.** Check pairs: $(2,3)=5$ but $(3,2)=6$. They differ, so **no, not symmetric**.

**Drill 17.** For symmetry, (row 2, col 1) must equal (row 1, col 2) $=7$. So $\square = 7$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 7 \\ 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Drill 18.** **Yes.** $I$ has all off-diagonal entries equal to $0$, so flipping across the diagonal changes nothing: $I^\top = I$.

:::
